attributes.stem: A 56-year-old woman presents with a firm swelling under the left side of her jaw, which has been present for 10 weeks (Image). The swelling is slowly getting larger, is not painful and does not change in size with meals. She is otherwise well and has not had any recent fevers, chills, night sweats, dental pain or respiratory tract symptoms. On examination the swelling is reddened in colour, firm, fixed and not tender. There is no facial asymmetry or weakness of the masseter muscles. Facial movements and sensations are normal. There are no other adjacent swellings. Her teeth appear sound on examination.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This swelling is most likely to be an enlarged lymph node. Soft, mobile and tender lymph nodes are likely to be benign. Firm, non-mobile, non-tender, slowly growing lymph nodes point towards malignancy. The next step in this patient's assessment must be an oropharyngeal examination. If this swelling is an indication of metastatic disease, the primary tumour is most likely to be in the mouth or pharynx.

Sialadenitis more commonly affects the parotid gland than submandibular gland. Acute sialadenitis is commonly due to bacterial or viral infections and presents with systemic toxicity, rapid onset painful unilateral erythematous salivary gland swelling with purulent discharge. Chronic sialadenitis is usually secondary to obstruction from calculi or a stricture presenting with swelling without erythema, causing repeated pain, often with meals. It is related to decreased salivary flow (dehydration, antihistamines) and calculi (sialolithiasis). Classical autoimmune sialadenitis (e.g., Sjogren syndrome) typically affects salivary and lacrimal glands and occurs in peri/post-menopausal women. It presents as persistent, usually asymptomatic bilateral swelling of the parotid glands, dry eyes, dry mouth and a history of concomitant connective tissue disease (e.g., SLE, RA). This is little to support such a diagnosis in this case.

A branchial cyst may present as a painless, fluctuant neck mass and whilst they are congenital in origin, they may not become evident until the patient is in their third or fourth decade. The cysts lie immediately anterior to the sternocleidomastoid. In other words, the site and physical characteristics of the swelling in this patient would exclude a branchial cyst.

Epidermoid cysts are the most common type of cutaneous cyst, predominantly affecting males and occurring in the third and fourth decades of life. They are lined by stratified squamous epithelium and have a keratin plug. Most cases are sporadic; however, genetic disorders such as Gardner syndrome, pachyonychia congenita type 2 and basal cell naevus syndrome increase the risk of multiple epidermoid cysts. They present as a slow-growing, mobile, firm and painless flesh-coloured nodule and are usually much smaller than the swelling present in this patient. A central punctum is usually visible. These cysts are usually asymptomatic but can become infected, painful and discharge their contents. Epidermoid cysts have a predilection for the face, head, neck, back or genitals. The lesion in this patient does not have the appearance of an epidermoid cyst.

A submandibular abscess usually occurs from an odontogenic infection (especially the second and third mandibular molars). Other causes may be a long-standing carious tooth with a slow-growing abscess that escapes the mandibular bone into the submandibular space. Some risk factors include trauma (such as fractures of the mandible), poor dental care and tooth extractions. Symptoms of a submandibular abscess include pain in the teeth, a reddened and tender submandibular induration or swelling, and fever and tachycardia may be present. Additionally, there may be symptoms from laryngeal oedema, such as trismus, dysphagia or drooling. Refuting features for a submandibular abscess include the lack of fever, tooth pain and a chronic timeline (in the case it is from an acute odontogenic infection). Although a slow growing abscess is possible, the lack of tooth pain and fever still negates this.
learningPoints: Slow growing, painless swellings under the jaw must alway raise the possibility of an underlying malignancy.
references: Zito PM, Scharf R. Epidermoid cyst. StatPearls [Internet]. StatPearls Publishing; 2021.

Adhikari R, Soni A. Submandibular Sialadenitis And Sialadenosis. [Updated 2022 Aug 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK562211/
Fox R. I. (2005). Sjögren's syndrome. Lancet (London, England), 366(9482), 321–331. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(05)66990-5
